title: Livonia (village), New York

infobox.value: is a [village](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_New_York#Village "Administrative divisions of New York") in the [town of Livonia](/wiki/Livonia_(town),_New_York "Livonia (town), New York"), [Livingston County](/wiki/Livingston_County,_New_York "Livingston County, New York"), [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), United States. [U.S. Route 20A](/wiki/U.S._Route_20A_(New_York) "U.S. Route 20A (New York)") and Route 15 intersect in the village. The population was 1,409 at the 2010 census,[[2]](#cite_note-Census_2010-2) out of 7,809 in the entire town.

**Livonia** is a [village](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_New_York#Village "Administrative divisions of New York") in the [town of Livonia](/wiki/Livonia_(town),_New_York "Livonia (town), New York"), [Livingston County](/wiki/Livingston_County,_New_York "Livingston County, New York"), [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), United States. [U.S. Route 20A](/wiki/U.S._Route_20A_(New_York) "U.S. Route 20A (New York)") and Route 15 intersect in the village. The population was 1,409 at the 2010 census,[[2]](#cite_note-Census_2010-2) out of 7,809 in the entire town.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Livonia_(village),_New_York&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Geography")]

Livonia is located in northeastern Livingston County at [42°49′12″N 77°40′5″W﻿ / ﻿42.82000°N 77.66806°W﻿ / 42.82000; -77.66806](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Livonia_(village),_New_York&params=42_49_12_N_77_40_5_W_type:city) (42.820009, -77.668176),[[3]](#cite_note-GR1-3) slightly west of the geographic center of the town of Livonia. Unincorporated [Livonia Center](/wiki/Livonia_Center,_New_York "Livonia Center, New York") is 1.5 miles (2.4 km) to the east, and [Lakeville](/wiki/Lakeville,_New_York "Lakeville, New York"), another unincorporated hamlet in the town, is 2 miles (3 km) to the west on Route 20A. [Geneseo](/wiki/Geneseo_(village),_New_York "Geneseo (village), New York"), the Livingston [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat"), is 9 miles (14 km) to the west, and [Rochester](/wiki/Rochester,_New_York "Rochester, New York") is 25 miles (40 km) to the north.

According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the village has a total area of 1.0 square mile (2.6 km2), all land.[[4]](#cite_note-Gazetteer_2019-4) Wilkins Creek, a tributary of [Conesus Lake](/wiki/Conesus_Lake "Conesus Lake") to the west, flows across the southwestern part of the village, and Little Conesus Creek rises in the northern part of the village. The entire village is within the [Genesee River](/wiki/Genesee_River "Genesee River") watershed.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Livonia_(village),_New_York&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

The region was the territory for centuries of the [Seneca people](/wiki/Seneca_people "Seneca people"), one of the Five Nations of the [Iroquois](/wiki/Iroquois "Iroquois") confederacy. They named the lake *Ga-ne-a-sos*, meaning "Berry Place". It was transliterated into English as "Conesus."

Solomon Woodruff, a native of [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut"), settled here in 1789 as the first European American in the area, following the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"). The British ceded their territory to the United States without consulting with the Iroquois nations. Many of the Iroquois Native Americans were forced violently from the state, and settled primarily on a major reserve in [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario").

The Seneca had to contend with Yankee settlers encroaching on their lands. Woodruff built a rough cabin, cleared a field, and planted his first crop of potatoes before returning to Connecticut for his family. He returned by oxcart with his wife to find that the Seneca had burned the cabin to the ground. (Their son died on the journey and was buried on a hilltop in [Bristol, New York](/wiki/Bristol,_New_York "Bristol, New York").) Woodruff rebuilt the cabin, and some of his descendants still reside in the area. He was buried in the Union cemetery.

The town developed between [Conesus](/wiki/Conesus_Lake "Conesus Lake") and [Hemlock](/wiki/Hemlock_Lake "Hemlock Lake") lakes, and was largely settled in the mid-19th century by ethnic European Americans from New England and New York. The construction of a railway line in the 1850s to Lakeville increased business opportunities and travel through the region.

The [Livonia Baptist Church](/wiki/Livonia_Baptist_Church "Livonia Baptist Church") was added to the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") in 1977.[[5]](#cite_note-nris-5)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Livonia_(village),_New_York&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Demographics")]

Historical population

| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [1880](/wiki/1880_United_States_census "1880 United States census") | 688 |  | — |
| [1890](/wiki/1890_United_States_census "1890 United States census") | 738 |  | 7.3% |

| [1910](/wiki/1910_United_States_census "1910 United States census") | 823 |  | −4.9% |

| [2020](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census") | 1,472 |  | 4.5% |
| U.S. Decennial Census[[6]](#cite_note-DecennialCensus-6) | | | |

As of the [census](/wiki/Census "Census")[[7]](#cite_note-GR2-7) of 2000, there were 1,373 people, 534 households, and 370 families residing in the village. The population density was 1,343.5 inhabitants per square mile (518.7/km2). There were 560 housing units at an average density of 548.0 per square mile (211.6/km2). The racial makeup of the village was 97.31% [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._Census) "White (U.S. Census)"), 0.87% [Black](/wiki/African_American_(U.S._Census) "African American (U.S. Census)") or [African American](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), 0.36% [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._Census) "Native American (U.S. Census)"), 0.22% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._Census) "Asian (U.S. Census)"), 0.07% from [other races](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), and 1.17% from two or more races. [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_(U.S._Census) "Hispanic (U.S. Census)") or [Latino](/wiki/Latino_(U.S._Census) "Latino (U.S. Census)") of any race were 1.02% of the population.

There were 534 households, out of which 40.3% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 51.9% were [married couples](/wiki/Marriage "Marriage") living together, 13.7% had a female householder with no husband present, and 30.7% were non-families. 25.5% of all households were made up of individuals, and 11.0% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.57 and the average family size was 3.09.

In the village, the population was spread out, with 30.6% under the age of 18, 6.6% from 18 to 24, 30.0% from 25 to 44, 22.3% from 45 to 64, and 10.5% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 35 years. For every 100 females, there were 94.5 males. For every 100 females age 18 and over, there were 89.1 males.

The median income for a household in the village was $49,688, and the median income for a family was $55,096. Males had a median income of $41,310 versus $25,069 for females. The [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") for the village was $21,115. About 3.5% of families and 6.0% of the population were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line "Poverty line"), including 9.9% of those under age 18 and 6.5% of those age 65 or over.
